ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are extensively applied for bioimaging due to their unique photoluminescence properties, while less attention has been paid to their inherent bio-functions as targeted drug delivery vehicles. To address this limitation, a novel CQDs-based drug delivery system (Arg-Ag@Cu) was constructed using l-arginine as precursor with duplex metal co-doping (Ag&Cu) for further DOX-loading/releasing and synergistic tumor theranostics. Specifically, the functional residues retention ability of CQDs guaranteed the reactive nitrogen species (RNS) replenishment, Ag-doping enhanced the photoluminescence intensity, and Cu2+-functionalizing could act as the anchor for further interparticle self-assembly, resulting in intelligent adjustable CQDs size for increasing the drug-loading capacity. After reaching targeted sites, CQDs could act as NO/ONOO− donors for gas-involving therapy, and Cu2+ could not only generate ROS through chemodynamic therapy (CDT), but also modulate the band structure of as-prepared CQDs for laser-induced photodynamic therapy (PDT) to further replenishing ROS. Simultaneously, with Cu2+ depletion, the structure of Arg-Ag@Cu was disrupted, which guaranteed in situ DOX-releasing. As such, these orchestrated procedures fully exerted the intrinsic bio-functions of CQDs then act as adjuvants in drug delivery systems, thus representing synergistic effects in suppressing tumor progression and migration with minimal side effects, and provides a paradigm of potent CQDs-based nanotheranostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an emergent class of carbonaceous materials with high biocompatibility and strong photoluminescence properties, which have been widely used for bioimaging and biosensing [1–5]. Recently, CQDs have gained interest in the field of drug delivery system [6]. Besides utilizing their fluorescent properties to visualize the drug accumulation and activities at pathological sites [7], using CQDs as drug carriers could also improve the solubility of water-insoluble drugs, improve their biocompatibility, and increase their cellular uptake [8], thereby enhancing their therapeutic effects. In addition, CQDs per se have the potential for the therapeutic of various diseases, especially cancer, and are expected to become adjuvants for existing therapeutic methods, simultaneously, which could produce “a whole greater than the sum of the parts” effect in precision medicine [9–11]. However, the applications of CQDs in the field of drug delivery and cancer theranostics still suffered from the selection of carbon sources to finely regulate CQDs properties, and the uncontrollable ultrasmall particle size [12].

Given these limitations, most of the studies are still focusing on the photoluminescence properties of CQDs, while only a few are focusing on their performances as drug delivery vehicles as well as the therapeutic effects of CQDs themselves. It is regrettable to overlook the potential multi-functional therapeutic performances of CQDs. Specifically, the bio-functions of CQDs were mainly guaranteed by the intrinsic inherited factors (carbon sources and dopants) [6] and the extrinsic functionalization (rational self-assembly) [13]. The intrinsic inherited bio-functions of CQDs were guaranteed by the synthetic precursors. Specifically, using functional molecules as precursors, the as-synthesized CQDs possess similar or superior therapeutic performances as compared to pristine agents due to the retention of functional groups in their structures [1]. More importantly, metallic dopants can chelate with residues on the surface of CQDs, subsequently modulate the band structure of CQDs, thus enhancing photoluminescence ability [14]. As such, CQDs have enormous potential to display multitudinous therapeutic performances [15–17]. As for the extrinsic functionalization, the abundant chemically reactive groups such as carboxyl, amidogen, or other components affixed to the surface of the CQDs could not only bond with multitudinous biological substances [18] to attain emerging therapeutic performance for a wide range of biological application [19], but also act as the anchor for further interparticle self-assembly, thus regulating the morphology and particle size of CQDs [20,21]. In this regard, the emerging biological functions as well as the intelligent adjustable size of as-prepared CQDs could be achieved by artificially regulating the extent of extrinsic functionalization, which has the potential to enhance the therapeutic effect while increasing the drug loading capacity, thus addressing the limitations of CQDs as drug delivery systems.

According to the above-mentioned intrinsic factors on the bio-functionalize of CQDs, the selection of functional carbon sources is of crucial [22]. l-arginine (L-Arg), a natural nitric oxide (NO) donor with excellent biocompatibility, can constantly release NO in the presence of nitric oxide synthase (NOS), followed by interacting with reactive oxygen species (ROS) thus yielding peroxynitrite (ONOO−) [23]. During this process, the guanidine group of L-Arg plays a crucial role [24]. Considering the bio-functional residues retention of precursors by CQDs, L-Arg-based CQDs could act as NO donors for gas-involving theranostics of cardiovascular disease and cancer (this part of work has been submitted). In particular, NO and ONOO− are more generally known as reactive nitrogen species (RNS), which represent a novel approach to controlling tumor progression through inducing necrosis or apoptosis [25–27]. Specifically, NO has been widely reported to down-regulated matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2 and MMP-9), which is closely related to tumor invasion and metastasis [28–30]. Moreover, NO has a strong vasodilation effect, benefiting the augment of the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect of nanoparticles by selectively dilating tumor blood vessels as the endothelium-derived relaxing factor [31]. Therefore, the construction of L-Arg-based CQDs could not only guarantee the in situ generation of RNS in the tumor tissue to suppress tumor progression and metastasis, but also dilate blood vessels to increase vascular permeability, which is beneficial to CQDs as drug delivery systems for further targeted tumor therapy. However, the photoluminescence intensity of solitary L-Arg-based CQDs was barely satisfactory. At this point, the first thought is to conduct metal doping. Considering the effect of metallic dopants on the fluorescence properties of CQDs [32], silver (Ag)-doping has the potential to enhance the photoluminescence intensity of pristine L-Arg CQDs. During the carbonization, the doping of Ag could chelation with chemical groups in L-Arg, then changing the electron distribution and surface structure of the synthesized CQDs, along with adjusting the gap between the conduction band and valence band thus improving their fluorescent properties [33].

Concurrently, the abundant groups on the surface of CQDs could ensure the extrinsic functionalization for emerging biological activity while intelligently regulating the morphology and particle size of as-prepared CQDs by rational self-assembly. For example, the residual amidogen groups on the surface of CQDs could bind with Cu (II) to form copper amine bonds [34,35], and act as anchor sites for further agglomeration and growth during functionalization. As such, controllable self-assembly and intelligently coordinated particle size can be achieved by regulating the extent of Cu (II) functionalization. Since copper ions have switchable valence states Cu (II) and Cu (I), they could mimic functions of native peroxidase to exert ROS-based chemodynamic therapy (CDT) through Fenton-like reaction thus were extensively applied in tumor therapy [36–38]. Studies have also reported that Cu (II) can act as endogenous triggers to achieve effective release of NO in a specific organization [39,40]. These excellent properties lead us to use Cu (II) for the functionalization of CQDs for further ROS-based tumor therapy while adjunctively exerting the bio-functions of L-Arg, simultaneously. Despite the extensive positive effect of Cu (II) for functionalization, Cu (II)-based therapy is still suffering from severe queries as promoting the formation of new blood vessels through copper signaling [41], even inducing tumor metastasis [42]. As mentioned above, sufficient NO in lesion location is expected to inhibit tumor migration, therefore, it would be a promising approach to avert the metastasis caused by Cu (II).

Therefore, in this article, a duplex metal co-doped CQDs-based drug delivery platform (Arg-Ag@Cu) with intelligent adjustable particle size was elaborated by doping Ag/Cu (II) into L-Arg-based CQDs (see Scheme 1 ). Specifically, the doped-Ag during the synthesis process boosted the fluorescence intensity of emitted light, and the as-prepared CQDs could not only inherit the RNS replenish ability of L-Arg, but also push on the ROS-based CDT effect for anti-tumor therapy through the functionalization by Cu (II)-doping. In addition, the metallic dopant could modulate the band structure of CQDs, resulting in an emerging absorbance peak around 650 nm, which guaranteed the 660 nm-induced photodynamic therapy (PDT), and artificially adjust the particle size, simultaneously. Moreover, using as-prepared CQDs as a drug delivery platform for carrying clinical standard chemotherapeutic agent doxorubicin (DOX) could increase its water solubility and bioavailability, and the CDT effect of Cu (II) could consume GSH thus offsetting its detoxication and reparation of cell damage caused by chemotherapy drug, and relieve chemotherapy resistance further [43]. After reaching targeted sites, along with the depletion of Cu (II), the structure of CQDs was disrupted, which guaranteed situ DOX-releasing. Interestingly, not only can Cu (II) ions act as an endogenous trigger for RNS production, but the supplemented NO-based RNS can also simultaneously reverse copper signaling induced tumor metastasis. Furthermore, the CDT effect of Cu (II) could enhance the PDT effect by supplementing ample O2 while the laser-induced PDT could in turn assist the CDT-supplied ROS. On this basis, coating Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX with M1 macrophage-derived microvesicles (MMVs) to construct a biomimetic agent could further improve the targeting effect on tumor areas and achieve a specific localization and sustained release (Scheme 1).

Silver nitrate (AgNO3) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, America). l-arginine (L-Arg) was obtained from Huaxia Reagent (Chengdu, China). Copper (II) nitrate trihydrate was purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, America). 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescin, diacetate (DCFH-DA), 3-Amino, 4-aminomethyl-2′, 7′-difluorescein, diacetate (DAF-FM DA), 3,3′-dioctadecyloxacarbocyanine perchlorate (DiO), Griess Reagent, Reactive Oxygen Species Assay Kit, Coomassie Bright Blue Superfast Dye, Crystal Violet Staining Solution, 2-(4-Amidinophenyl)-6-indolecarbamidine dihydrochloride (DAPI dihydrochloride) and Annexin V-FITC/PI Kit were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology (Beijing, China). 1, 3-Diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, America). Methylene blue (MB) was obtained from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was obtained from Gibco (Waltham, America). ONOO− Detection Kit (Cat: HR8843) was purchased from Biolab (Beijing, China). Glutathione (GSH) and 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were obtained from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) and Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium Nutriont Mixture F-12 (Ham) (DMEM/F-12) were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Waltham, America). Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was purchased from ExcellBio (Shanghai, China). Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) was obtained from Sango Biotech (Shanghai, China). Transwell Chamber with 8 μm pore diameter (Corning Incorporated, America). 4% Paraformaldehyde Fix Solution were obtained from BBI Life Sciences (Shanghai, China). Anti-mouse antibodies for flow cytometry (anti-CD45-PE/Cy7, anti-F4/80-FITC, anti-CD80-APC) were from Biolegend (San Diego, America). The rabbit monoclonal β-actin, caspase-3, Bax, Bcl-2, MMP-2, MMP-9, TIMP-1, TIMP-2, CD44, α4 antibodies and Cytochalasin B actin polymerization (CB) were purchased from Abcam (Cambridge, UK). Horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibody, BeyoECL Moon highly sensitive ECL chemiluminescence kit and skimmed milk powder were obtained from Beyotime Biotechnology (Beijing, China). Doxorubicin (DOX) and Indocyanine Green (ICG) were purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dialysis Membrane was bought from Solarbio (Beijing, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and acetic acid were obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). The aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) detection kit were purchased from Mindray Bio-Medical Electronics Co., Ltd (Shenzhen, China). The water used in this work was produced by an ultra-pure-water purification system of Research Water Purification Technology Co., Ltd (Xiamen, China).

Murine breast cancer 4T1 cell line (4T1 cells) was obtained from Shanghai Institute of Cells, Chinese Academy of Sciences. The murine macrophage cell line Raw 264.7 and human umbilical vein endothelial cells (HUVEC) were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA). The mice used in the experiment were obtained from Hunan SJA Laboratory Animal Co., Ltd. And the animal experimental protocols were approved by the Ethics Committee of Chongqing Medical University (SCXK2018-0003).

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption was collected on Model Ultra-6600A (Rigol, China). The PL spectra were recorded by a RF-5301 spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was measured by the Nicolet iS5 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). The basic morphology features and element mapping of the as-prepared nanomaterials were characterized by JEM-1200EX TEM (JEOL, Japan) operating at an acceleration voltage of 120 kV (carbon-coated copper grid was dipped in the sample solution to deposit it on the film). Zeta potentials and DLS were analyzed by ZEN3600 (Malvern Instruments Ltd, England). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Oxygen production experiment was employed by JPBJ-608 portable dissolved oxygen analyzer (INESA, China). Intracellular fluorescence intensity was obtained on Nikon Eclipse Ti–S inverted fluorescence microscope (Nikon Corporation, Japan) and confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (Leica, Germany). MTT assay results were analyzed using Varioskan LUX microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific, America). Cellular uptake, detection of ONOO− and apoptosis experiment were analyzed by CytoFLEX S (Beckman Coulter, Inc., America). The light source was acquired by a LWRL660 (Laserwave Co., Ltd., China). The instruments used in western blot analysis were all from Bio-Rad, USA. The specific proteins were visualized using an automatic chemiluminescence analysis system named Tanon-5200 (Tanon Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China) in western blot analysis. In vivo targeting of as-prepared CQDs was studied using VISQUE Invivo Smart (Vieworks, Korea). The infrared imaging systems were purchased from FOTROC (Shanghai, China). The levels of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) were measured by Mindray BS-220 (Mindray Bio-Medical Electronics Co., Ltd, China).

The Arg-Ag CQDs were prepared according to the reported method and further improved [44]. Briefly, Arg-Ag CQDs were synthesized using L-Arg and AgNO3 as raw materials using a simple hydrothermal reaction. First of all, L-Arg (0.8 g) and AgNO3 (0.08 g) were dissolved in ultra-pure water (20 mL). Then, transferred the aqueous solution to a 30 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The mixture was then heated at 180 °C for 6 h in a muffle furnace (Nabertherm, China). After naturally cooled to room temperature, the aqueous solution was further centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min to remove the incompletely reacted impurities, and separated the supernatant to obtain crude Arg-Ag CQDs aqueous solution, followed by purifying using dialysis membrane (MWCO 500 D) against water for 48 h (the synthesis of Arg CQDs was similar to Arg-Ag CQDs, without adding AgNO3). 20 mM Cu(NO3)2 aqueous solution was added dropwise to the above supernatant, and the mixture was heated at 50 °C for 30 min to obtain crude Arg-Ag@Cu solution after the assembly process. After that, the crude product was purified by extensive dialysis against distilled water (MWCO 1000 D) for 24 h to remove free copper ions. The concentration of Arg-Ag@Cu solution was determined by theoretical calculation after freeze-drying. Specially, Arg-Ag@Cu used in this following experiment is about 34.17 mg/mL. Herein, the fluorescence of previous Arg-Ag CQDs was quenched during the process of assembly process (the details were showed in Supporting Information).

To evaluate the stability in aqueous solution, the as-prepared Arg-Ag@Cu (CArg-Ag@Cu = 34.17 mg/mL) was dispersed in ultrapure water, PBS, and cell culture medium (DMEM/F12 with 10%FBS) (CArg-Ag@Cu = 17.09 mg/mL) for 24 h, and then observed the fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and the particle size of Arg-Ag@Cu.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro validation of photothermal effect
ce:section.ce:para: Arg-Ag@Cu aqueous solution with different concentrations (500 μg/mL, 1 mg/mL, and 2 mg/mL) was irradiated under 660 nm laser (1A, 349 mW/cm2), followed by detecting the temperature every 2 min using an infrared thermal imaging camera.

60 μL of H2O2 (1 M) was added to 3 mL of Arg-Ag@Cu aqueous solution (200 μg/mL) under consistently stirring. Then, the concentration of generated O2 was monitored by dissolved oxygen analyzer and REXDC1.1 data acquisition software. H2O2 aqueous solution was used as the control.

DPBF was used to measure the generation of extracellular 1O2. In a typical process, H2O2 (1 M, 0.5 μL), GSH (10 mM, 100 μL) and DPBF ethanol solution (10 mM, 20 μL) were dispersed in 2 mL of Arg-Ag@Cu solution (200 μg/mL, Vethanol: Vwater = 6:4). Then the mixtures were irradiated under 660 nm laser (1A, 349 mW/cm2). The generation of 1O2 was measured by UV–vis spectroscopy (λ = 420 nm).

To test the extracellular ·OH generation, MB was applied as the indicator of ·OH elicited by Arg-Ag CQDs or Arg-Ag@Cu with H2O2. Arg-Ag@Cu (34.17 mg/mL) 100 μL was mixed with an MB solution (10 μg/mL, 100 μL) for 1 h in the dark to establish an adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Then, H2O2 (1 M, 2 μL) and GSH (10 mM, 100 μL) were added to the mixture and then exposed to 660 nm laser (1A, 349 mW/cm2). After 10 min irradiation, the MB aqueous solution was centrifuged and the supernatant was measured by UV–vis spectroscopy (λ = 665 nm).

4T1 cells were cultured in the DMEM/F-12 medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin/streptomycin (37 °C, 5% CO2). HUVEC and RAW 264.7 cells were cultured in the DMEM medium with the same condition.

The cytotoxicity studies of Arg-Ag@Cu on 4T1 and HUVEC were assessed by MTT. Firstly, cells were seeded in the 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). After that, the medium was replaced by a medium containing different concentration of Arg-Ag@Cu (predetermined concentration of 0, 5, 10, 20, 50,100, 200, 500 and 1000 μg/mL) or 10% DMSO (positive control), and then incubated for another 24 h. Next, the medium in each well was replaced by 100 μL of fresh culture medium which contained 5 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL). After incubation for another 4 h, the supernatant was discarded, and 150 μL DMSO was added to each well. Then, the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. At last, cell viability was evaluated by measuring the absorbance of the reaction products at 570 nm using a microplate reader. Every experiment was conducted three times.

Flow cytometry: 4T1 cells (1 × 105 cells/well, seeded in 48-well plates) were plated 36 h prior to incubation with Arg-Ag@Cu (200 μg/mL). At predetermined time points (0 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h, 24 h and 36 h), the cells were washed by PBS three times, trypsinized, and resuspended in PBS. The cellular uptake behavior was then measured by flow cytometry.

CLSM: 4T1 cells (1 × 106 per well) were seeded into a 24-well plate containing sterile glass cover slip and cultured for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2), then incubated with Arg-Ag@Cu (200 μg/mL). At predetermined time points (0 h, 2 h, 4 h, 8 h, 12 h, 24 h and 36 h), the cells were washed by PBS three times and fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde. Then, the fluorescence intensity of Arg-Ag@Cu was observed under CLSM (λex = 405 nm, λem = 460–500 nm).

4T1 cells (5 × 105 per well) were seeded into a 6-well plate for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, each well was treated with free PBS, Arg-Ag@Cu, Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2, Arg-Ag@Cu + Laser and Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + Laser for 12 h at 37 °C. The indicated groups were exposed to a 660 nm laser (1A, 349 mW/cm2) for 10 min. After that, the media was removed followed by rinsing with PBS three times. Then, the cells were lysed for 2 min, and the suspended lysate was transferred into a 96 well plate (50 μL per well for 3 wells). Finally, 50 μL Griess Reagent I and Griess Reagent II were added successively. At last, the UV–vis absorption at 540 nm was evaluated.

DAF-FM DA was also used as the indicator of intracellular NO. 4T1 cells (1 × 106 per well) were seeded into a 24-well plate containing sterile glass cover slip and cultured for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, each well was treated with free PBS, Arg-Ag@Cu, Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2, Arg-Ag@Cu + Laser and Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + Laser (λex = 660 nm, 1A, 349 mW/cm2, 10 min) for 12 h at 37 °C. After that, each well was rinsed by PBS three times. Then, DAF-FM DA solution (5 μM) prepared with dedicated DAF-FM DA diluent was added to each well for another 20 min incubation. At last, the 4T1 cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde. The intensity of red fluorescence (λex = 495 nm, λem = 515 nm) was investigated under IFM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ONOO− fluorescent probe for ONOO− detection
ce:section.ce:para: 4T1 cells were seeded in a 48-well plate (1 × 105 cells per well) and treated with free PBS, Arg-Ag@Cu, Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2, Arg-Ag@Cu + Laser and Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + Laser (λex = 660 nm, 349 mW/cm2, 10 min) for 12 h at 37 °C. Then the cells were incubated with ONOO− detection kit (Cat: HR8843) for 30 min and washed by PBS three times. At last, the cells were trypsinized and the fluorescence intensity of ONOO− detection probe in each group was analyzed by flow cytometry (FITC channel).

DCFH-DA was used as the probe to detect intracellular ROS. 1 × 106 4T1 cells per well were seeded into a 24-well plate containing sterile glass cover slip and cultured for 12 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, cells were incubated with H2O2 (200 μM), GSH (10 mM), and with/without Arg-Ag@Cu (200 μg/mL) for 12 h. Next, the cells in indicated groups were exposed to 660 nm laser (1A, 349 mW/cm2) for 10 min. After that, the cells were washed with PBS twice and incubated with DCFH-DA solution (10 μM) prepared with serum-free DMEM at 37 °C for another 30 min. Finally, the 4T1 cells were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde. The fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA (λex = 488 nm, λem = 520–580 nm) was observed under CLSM and further quantified by Image J software.

To investigate the apoptosis of 4T1 cells induced by Arg-Ag@Cu, Annexin V-FITC and PI were used to double stain 4T1 cells. The cells were seeded in a 48-well plate at a density of 1 × 105 cells per well and treated with free PBS, Arg-Ag@Cu, Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2, Arg-Ag@Cu + Laser and Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + Laser for 12 h at 37 °C. The cells were trypsinized and resuspended in PBS for staining of Annexin V-FITC and PI according to the manufacturer's protocol. Stained cells were collected and analyzed by flow cytometry.

Western blot analysis was used to investigate the expression of caspase 3, Bax, Bcl-2, MMP-2, MMP-9, TIMP-1 and TIMP-2, and performed as previously described [45]. Briefly, the cell lysates were centrifuged at 10000 g for 15 min at 4 °C. Then, the protein was separated by 6–12% sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (SDS-PAGE) and was transferred into the polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane (Beyotime, Shanghai, China). After blocked with TBST containing 5% skim milk, PVDF membrane was incubated with the primary antibody at 4 °C overnight. Then, the blots were washed three times by TBST, followed by incubation with the appropriate horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibody for 60 min. The specific proteins of each group were visualized using an automatic chemiluminescence analysis system. The intensity of the blots was quantified by Image J software.

Firstly, DOX (1 mg/mL, 1 mL) or ICG (1 mg/mL, 1 mL) was dissolved in dichloromethane and added into 4 mL Arg-Ag@Cu solution, followed by sonication in cold-water bath. And then, the dichloromethane was gradually evaporated, yielding the limpid CQDs/DOX nanoparticles in water solution. After stirring at room temperature for 24 h, the supernatant was centrifuged to remove impurities, and then the crude product was isolated by consistent dialysis against distilled water (MWCO 1000 D) for 24 h (pH = 7). In addition, the standard curve of DOX was established by the UV absorption peaks (λ = 550 nm) at a wide concentration range from 25 to 500 μg/mL.

The discrimination of macrophages phenotype was constructed according to the reported method [46]. Briefly, Raw 246.7 cells (1 × 105 cells/well, seeded in 48-well plates) were trypsinized, centrifuged, and resuspended in the antibody mix (100 μL CD45, F4/80 or CD80 dilutions) for 20 min at 4 °C in the dark for cell surface staining. After washing by staining buffer, the sample was analyzed by flow cytometry.

MMVs were prepared according to a previously reported process with some modifications [47]. Briefly, the M1 macrophages were rinsed twice by fresh PBS and then incubated with 10 mL serum-free DMEM culture medium which contained 10 μg/mL CB (37 °C, 5% CO2, 1 h). Then, the cells and MMVs were transferred into a 50 mL plastic centrifuge tube and continuously vibrate for 5 min. After that, 10 mL of FBS was added to the tube to ensure the final FBS concentration was 50%. Then, in order to remove cells and large MMVs aggregates, the suspension was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min followed by centrifuging the supernatant at 4000 rpm for 20 min to collect purified MMVs. The collected MMVs were then washed twice with deionized water (containing 0.25% EDTA) to remove cytoplasm, and the prepared MMVs were stored at 4 °C for later use.

Arg-Ag@Cu was mixed with the MMVs and pulsed sonicated for 5 min using a water bath sonicator (SCIENTZ, China) at a power of 100 W to obtain crude MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu. The purification of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu was performed by centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 20 min to remove free MMVs. After centrifuged at 15000 g for 30 min, the MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu was collected and then lysed for SDS-PAGE electrophoresis and western blot analysis. Each sample was measured on a 12% SDS-PAGE at 120 V for 1 h, then Coomassie Blue staining and imaging were performed to visualize the protein composition of MMVs and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu. Using WB to visualized and quantified the expression of CD44 and α4. Total M1 macrophages were used as a control for analysis.

The verification of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX was constructed by DiO staining and further observing by CLSM. The DiO staining solution (5 μM, 500 μL) was added to isolated MMVs, and incubated for 15 min at 37 °C. Followed by rinsing twice by fresh PBS, the Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX was added to MMVs (DiO) and underwent the same synthesized and purified procedures as mentioned above to obtained MMV (DiO)-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX. The colocalization of green fluorescence signal (indicated MMVs) with red fluorescence signal (indicated Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX) was then observed by CLSM (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–550 nm).

The release of DOX from Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX was evaluated by a simple dialysis method using PBS buffer with different conditions as the medium. Briefly, 1.0 mL of each formulation containing 0.4236 mg DOX was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 D). The dialytic devices were put in 10 mL PBS with different conditions, the simulated TME condition (pH = 5, GSH = 10 mM, H2O2 = 200 μM) or the neutral condition (pH = 7). At predetermined time points, 1 mL release medium was collected followed by replenishing with an equal volume of fresh medium. Then quantified the released DOX by the UV–vis absorption.

200 μL Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG or MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG was injected into tumor-bearing mice (n = 3/group) through the tail-vein. Each formulation was injected at an equivalent dose of 5 mg/kg (ICG/body weight). Then, the mice were subjected to a near-infrared (NIR) small animal imaging system to study its tumor target ability at indicated time points (0–36 h) after the injection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tumor therapeutic effect on 4T1 tumor-bearing mice
ce:section.ce:para: 2.0 × 106 4T1 cells suspended in 200 μL PBS was injected into the tail-vein of ICR mice to build a lung metastasis model. After 14 days, the mice were divided into five groups (n = 5/group) and treated by intravenous injection with (I) PBS, (II) DOX, (III) Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, (IV) MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, (V) MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX + Laser (λex = 660 nm, 349 mW/cm2, 10 min) at 2 days interval. The concentration of the indicated formulations was equaled with 5 mg/kg DOX. During the treatment, the mice weights were measured after each administration. After 14 days, the mice were sacrificed and their lungs were taken to evaluate the area of necrotic tissues on the surface. Then, the heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney of each mouse were collected for the next evaluation.

The major organs (heart, liver, spleens, lung, kidney) were fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde solution and embedded in paraffin for tissue slices. The above slices were stained by hematoxylin and eosin (H&E), and the images were obtained by IFM.

Using a marker pen to draw a line every 0.5 cm on the back of the 6-well plate, at least five lines. Inoculating 4T1 cells (5 × 105 cells per well) into the 6-well plate (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then, the cells were incubated with Arg-Ag@Cu CQDs (200 μg/mL) for another 12 h. After that, each well was perpendicularly scraped by a sterile micropipette tip. The cells were rinsed with PBS three times, and serum-free DMEM/F12 culture medium was added into each well. Finally, images were acquired at predetermined time points (0, 6, 12, and 24 h) using IFM.

The effect of Arg-Ag@Cu on 4T1 cells migration was assessed using transwell assay. Briefly, 4T1 cells (1 × 105 cells per well) were inoculated into 24-well transwell upper chambers with 8 μm pore diameter. After incubation with Arg-Ag@Cu (200 μg/mL) for 12 h, the upper chambers were replaced by serum-free DMEM/F12, and 500 μl of the DMEM/F12 (10% FBS) was added into the lower chambers and then cultured for 24 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Cells migrated to the lower chambers were first fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde solution and then stained with 0.1% crystal violet staining solution. After destaining with 3% acetic acid, the crystal violet was completely removed and the eluate was taken for OD measurement (λ = 570 nm) on a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inhibition of tumor migration in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The tail-vein of ICR mice were injected with 2 × 106 4T1 cells suspended in 200 μL of PBS to build a lung metastasis model. After 5 days, the mice were divided into two groups (n = 5/group). Each group was intravenously injected with PBS or 200 μL Arg-Ag@Cu (200 μg/mL) every day for 9 days. Then, all of the mice were sacrificed and their lungs were taken to evaluate the area of necrotic tissue. At last, the lungs of mice in each group were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde for histological examination.

To further evaluate the safety of Arg-Ag@Cu, the serum of mice was collected after treatment, and the levels of aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT) were measured by using the standard kits according to the manufacturer's instructions.

Statistical analysis of all the experimental data was performed using Graph Pad Prism 6 software and the results are presented as the mean ± S. D. Student's t-test was used to compared differences between groups. The P-values < 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis and characterization of Arg-Ag@Cu
ce:section.ce:para: According to our concept, Arg-Ag@Cu is a novel duplex metal co-doped CQDs-based drug delivery system, which not only were expected to efficiently exert the superiority of functional precursors (Arg as the foundation of RNS replinishment while Ag as metalic dopant to enhance the photoluminescence capacity), but also possesses emerging bio-functions and intelligent adjustable particle size guararteed by the extent of functionalization by Cu (II) (through rational self-assembly). First, Arg-Ag CQDs was synthesized using L-Arg as the precursor and AgNO3 as the metal dopant using a simple hydrothermal reaction. Followed by Cu (II)-induced functionalization, the as-prepared CQDs could guarantee the abundant generation of both RNS and ROS in targeted area together with considerable drug-loading capacity to guarantee appreciable therapeutic effect. Fig. 1 A showed the absorption spectra of Arg-Ag CQDs before and after functionalized with Cu (II) ions. Arg-Ag CQDs exhibited a full-wave absorption, while Arg-Ag@Cu possessed a characteristic absorption peak at ∼650 nm, which is the UV–vis absorbance of cupric amine bonds [34,35]. The appearance of characteristic absorption peak and the following visible color change based on assembly indicated that cupric amine bonds connected Cu (II) with the amino residues on the surface of CQDs [48], contributed to the functionalization process. In addition, the absorbance peak around 650 nm was the foundation for subsequent 660 nm laser-triggered PDT effect. As for the tumor dynamic therapy (photodynamic therapy and chemodynamic therapy), the energy caused by electronic transition was essential. The energy required for the electronic transition of the as-prepared Arg-Ag@Cu, that is forbidden band width (Fig. S1A), was calculated by absorbance spectrum inserted in Fig. S1B (the specific calculation processes were shown in supporting information). The size of the forbidden band width is a physical quantity that reflects the strength of valence electrons being bound, that is, the minimum energy required to generate intrinsic excitation and electron transition. The relatively narrow band (2.6326 eV) could respond conveniently to the energy from laser (λex = 660 nm) and Cu (II) ions catalytic reaction for achieving photo-/chemo-dynamic therapy, respectively.

Using Ag as the metallic dopant during the synthesis process contributed to the formation of Arg-Ag CQDs with extremely strong photoluminescence (Fig. 1B), which was ∼45-fold stronger than Arg CQDS (λex = 300 nm) without Ag-doping (Fig. S2), and have tropism for in vivo and in vitro bioimaging. However, during the assembled process in water, the fluorescence intensity of initial Arg-Ag CQDs was decreasing accompanied by Cu (II) doping (Fig. 1C), which could guide the functionalization process. After mixed with 20 mM Cu (II) ions, as-prepared Arg-Ag@Cu emitted the similar wavelength-dependent luminescence (λex = 300–400 nm) mainly inherited from Arg-Ag CQDs, while the fluorescence dropped sharply (Fig. S3A). From the results of the standard curve (Fig. S3B), the fluorescence intensity was proportional to the logarithm value of the Cu (II) concentration (for the intuitive visualization of the correlation [49,50]) with a wide range from 0.5 μM to 20 mM (R2 = 0.99501).

The similar results can be observed in 4T1 cells (Figs. S3C and D), the ﬂuorescence intensity of Arg-Ag CQDs was gradually decreased along with the intracellular Cu (II) concentration increased from 0.1 to 1.0 mM. When the intracellular concentration of Cu (II) was 1.0 mM, the ﬂuorescence intensity of Arg-Ag CQDs was almost disappeared. These results indicated that Arg-Ag CQDs can also assemble with intracellular Cu (II). The fluorescence quenching processes could be explained by the complexolysis between Cu (II) and amino groups [34,35]. In particular, CQDs exhibit excitation dependent emission, and the quenching mechanism can be attributed to inner filter effect (IFE), interpreting as the absorption of excitation light and/or emission light by the absorber reduces the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore (Fig. S3E) [48]. As shown in the fluorescence spectrum, the UV–vis absorption band centered at 650 nm with the excitation and emission spectrum of Arg-Ag@Cu partially overlapped (Fig. S3F). Therefore, it can be speculated that the fluorescence attenuation of Arg-Ag CQDs by Cu (II) ions partly due to IFE, and could be calculated by using the method proposed by Leese and Wehry (showed in supporting information) [51]. By calculating, the fluorescence decrease caused by the IFE (FDIFE) is 26.89%. Since the human tumor tissues contain high concentrations of Cu (II) [52,53], several authors point to the central role played by Cu (II) in angiogenesis, a process critical for tumor progression, invasion, and metastasis [41]. They argued that the detection of Cu (II) in cancer-related cells such as Hela, A549, HepG2 or Hep-2 was beneficial to early diagnosis of diseases [54]. Therefore, the as-prepared CQDs can effectively respond to intracellular Cu (II), which is expected to be used as a promising agent for the early diagnosis of cancer.

The fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 1D) recorded in the range of 480–4000 cm−1 showed the surface functional groups of L-Arg, Arg-Ag CQDs and Arg-Ag@Cu, respectively. The IR peaks of the as-prepared Arg-Ag CQDs and Arg-Ag@Cu showed the distinct peaks at 1400 cm−1, 1650 cm−1, assigning to C–N, CN stretching vibrations. And the broad peaks from 3200 to 3700 cm−1 correspond to N–H and O–H stretching coming from the carboxylic acid and amine groups, which were similar to the characteristic peak of arginine's guanidine. Besides, the newly extra peak at about 1045 cm−1 emerged after functionalizing Arg-Ag CQDs with Cu (II), which is attributed to cupric amine bond stretching vibration [55,56]. On the basis of FTIR studies, Arg-Ag CQDs were verified to possess certain functional residues inherited from L-Arg, which could not only exhibit the biological functions of NO and ONOO− supplement, but also guarantee the further functionalization by Cu (II).

The basic morphology of Arg-Ag CQDs and Arg-Ag@Cu were measured by transmission electron microscope (TEM). Pure Arg-Ag CQDs exhibit a typical CQDs morphology with the average particle size of around 2 nm (Fig. 1E), and the hydrophilic Arg-Ag CQDs assembled into spheroidal clusters after doped with Cu (II) ions (Fig. 1F). In this process, the concentration of Cu (II) plays a decisive role on the particle size of as-prepared Arg-Ag@Cu clusters, and the size of CQDs-cluster can be tuned from ∼20 nm (CCu (II) = 10 mM) (Fig. S4) to ∼180 nm (CCu (II) = 20 mM) (Fig. 1F). The primary cause was the affinitive surface amidogen residues of Arg-Ag CQDs, which provide effective anchor sites for Cu (II) functionalization, and further promoting their agglomeration and growth. Such design is expected to solve the uncontrollable ultra-small particle size and inferior EPR effect of traditional CQDs. For the treatment of lung metastasis of breast cancer, the particle size of therapeutic agents in the range of 120–480 nm is proportional to the lung retention time [57]. Hence, the adjustable particle size by a certain extent of Cu (II) functionalization could also guarantee the retention time in lung to achieve effective treatment in following experimental procedures. As shown in Figs. S5A and C, the formation of the Arg-Ag CQDs and Arg-Ag@Cu was further confirmed by high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), respectively, which exhibited clear lattice fringes. The insets exhibited the distance of the lattice fringes of Arg-Ag CQDs (0.23 nm) and Arg-Ag@Cu (0.39 nm). The increase in lattice fringes distance might indicate the increase in amorphous domain attributed to the introduction of more defect site through doping of other elements in the crystal lattice. Figs. S5B and D represented the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the Arg-Ag CQDs and Arg-Ag@Cu. The concentric circles obtained in the SAED indicated that they were both polycrystalline in nature. The HRTEM results together with SAED confirmed the nanocrystalline nature of Arg-Ag CQDs and Arg-Ag@Cu. In order to execute the precise elemental distribution of Arg-Ag@Cu, the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS)-elemental mappings were displayed. As shown in Fig. 1G, carbon, oxygen, copper and silver distributed homogeneously on Arg-Ag@Cu. On the negative side, massive green and red dots covered the background of the image due to the interference of the copper mesh and the surface carbon film. Consistent with the results of TEM, the particle size of Arg-Ag@Cu detected by DLS was ∼180 nm (Fig. 1H), and the zeta potential of Arg-Ag CQDs and Arg-Ag@Cu were both negative (Fig. 1I). As previous inference, the lone-pair electrons transfer from amino to copper resulted in the increase of the surface charge of Arg-Ag@Cu compared with Arg-Ag CQDs (from −7.067 to −0.862 mV). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results further confirmed the chemical compositions of Arg-Ag@Cu (Fig. 1J). The oxygen atomic percentage was relatively high (20.67%), which was similar to the results of EDS-elemental mappings, indicating the abundant oxygenated groups on the surface of Arg-Ag@Cu (could be used to interpret the negative zeta potential). From Fig. S6A, the fitted Cu 2p3/2 peak showed a main signal at the binding energy of 934.7 eV, attributed to Cu (II) component in the Arg-Ag@Cu [58–60]. The Cu 3d orbitals were occupied by the common electron pair provided by the amino group [61]. In this process, Cu (II) obtained electrons thus became Cu (I), which explains the observed shoulder signal at 933.4 eV. There was a presence of a weak satellite peak at 943.8 eV that corresponds to the partly unoccupied Cu 3d orbitals. Fig. S6B showed the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Ag 3d. Two binding energy peaks at 367.80 eV and 373.8 eV belong to metallic Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2, respectively [62]. Figs. S6C–E showed the signal of the fitted C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peak at the binding energy of 284.8 eV, 399.7 eV and 531.2 eV, indicated the existence of C–C, CO, C–O, C–N, and N–H.

To evaluate the stability in aqueous solution, the as-prepared Arg-Ag@Cu was dispersed in ultrapure water, PBS and cell culture medium (DMEM/F12 with 10%FBS) for 24 h. Fig. S7 showed that the UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and the particle size of Arg-Ag@Cu did not significantly change before and after placement, indicated that Arg-Ag@Cu was stable in each solution and no significant agglomeration occurred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro anticancer effect of Arg-Ag@Cu
Cell uptake and cytotoxicity of Arg-Ag@Cu
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the anticancer effect of Arg-Ag@Cu in vitro, we first investigated the cellular uptake capacity of Arg-Ag@Cu measured by flow cytometry (FCM) and CLSM (Fig. S8). Arg-Ag@Cu was taken up the most by 4T1 cells at 12 h, which was used as the best incubation time for subsequent experiments. For cytotoxicity of Arg-Ag@Cu, cell viability of carcinoma cells (4T1 cells) and normal cells (HUVEC) after treated with Arg-Ag@Cu were investigated via MTT assay at predetermined time points (10% DMSO was used as the positive control). The cytotoxicity of Arg-Ag@Cu at various concentrations on 4T1 cells and HUVEC were displayed in Fig. S9. It can be seen that the survival rate of HUVEC remained above 90% even with the concentration of Arg-Ag@Cu reaching 500 μg/mL, while only 50% of 4T1 cells being remained alive after the treatment of Arg-Ag@Cu at 200 μg/mL, indicating higher cytotoxicity towards tumor cells than normal cells. These results are mainly attributed to the RNS/ROS-releasing under the specific TME conditions, leading to the difference in the viability of carcinoma cells and normal cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reactive nitrogen species (RNS) production in 4T1 cells
ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, a large number of studies have confirmed the positive effect of RNS in the treatment of tumors, including apoptosis [63], cytostasis [64], tumor metastasis inhibition [30] and EPR effect enhancement [31]. In the presence of nitric oxide synthase (NOS), the guanidine group of L-Arg can be spontaneously oxidized by H2O2 to produce primary RNS type, nitric oxide (NO) [23], and this reaction can be greatly enhanced under H2O2-rich TME conditions to contribute a large amount of intra-tumoral NO for gas therapy. In recent years, there have been increasing reports on exogenous stimuli such as laser [65,66], x-ray [67], and ultrasound [68,69] to induce NO release, and Cu (II) also could act as an endogenous trigger for NO production [39,40]. Considering the bio-activity retention of precursors by CQDs, the generation of NO by Arg-Ag@Cu necessitates targeted investigation. First, to determine whether the laser intensity used in the following experimental procedures could cause Arg-Ag@Cu nanocomplex to disassemble as a result of the photothermal effect, we acquired infrared thermal images of Arg-Ag@Cu under laser irradiation at different time points (Fig. S10). The results showed that even with the concentration of 2 mg/mL, Arg-Ag@Cu exhibited a lower temperature (40.4 °C, 10 min), indicating exposed at a laser at 660 nm with a power of 349 mW/cm2 could not engender significant photothermal effects satisfied for the disassembly of the aggregated Arg-Ag@Cu nanocomplex.

Then the laser-induced RNS generation capacity of Arg-Ag@Cu was subsequently verified, and the schematic diagram was shown in Fig. 2 A. The results obtained from the preliminary analysis by the classic Griess Reagent showed that Arg-Ag@Cu could produce NO in 4T1 cells (∼0.2 μM) (Fig. 2B). The production of NO augmented after the addition of H2O2 (∼0.28 μM), and then with exogenous stimuli such as laser irradiation, the concentration of NO hit the highest point (∼0.35 μM). The laser-enhanced NO production attributed to the energy of laser photons enhanced the electron transfer during the oxidation process of guanidine groups. The same results can be seen from IFM using NO fluorescent probe DAF-FM DA (Fig. 2C). The analysis of the mean PL intensity of the DAF-FM DA revealed that the combination treatment of H2O2 and laser led to a five-fold increase in the supplement of NO compared with Arg-Ag@Cu alone treatment (Fig. 2D). Several approaches have been devoted to enhancing the EPR effect due to its critical role in the treatment of tumor by nanomedicines [70]. Among them, increasing blood flow into tumors by vasculature modulation is the most widely studied. As a vasodilator, NO could induce blood vessel dilation, and increase blood flow into tumors thus enhancing the EPR effect of co-administrated nanomedicines [31]. Encouraged by above results, Arg-Ag@Cu was expected to further accumulate in the tumor sites through NO-enhanced EPR effect, which laid the foundation as the targeted drug delivery system.

Under TME condition, the reaction of NO with superoxide leads to the formation of another RNS, peroxynitrite (ONOO−), which is an extremely reactive nitrogen-centered radical with high oxidation and nitrification properties [71]. The formed ONOO− can attack a wide range of biomolecules, including cellular DNA and proteins, thus leading to cell apoptosis or necrosis [72,73]. Therefore, it is necessary to validate whether Arg-Ag@Cu can realize TME-response ONOO− generation, which has theoretic and realistic significance in the treatment of tumors. Similar to NO production, it can be seen from the results of FCM that Arg-Ag@Cu cannot induce the substantial generation of ONOO− in the presence or absence of laser irradiation without H2O2 (Fig. 2E), implying that H2O2 was necessitated for both NO and ONOO− supplement. Based on the quantitative analyses of the results from FCM (Fig. 2F), the PL intensity of ONOO− detection probe (indicated the production of ONOO−) showed a markedly increase from ∼230 K (Arg-Ag@Cu alone) to ∼ 350 K (Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + Laser). The results in this section indicated that focusing on the supplement of NO and its downstream nitrogen-centered radicals caused by intracellular Arg-Ag@Cu is a promising research direction, which may provide innovative ways for tumor treatment.

To further validate the assumption of Arg-Ag@Cu as a promising antitumor agent, the ROS produced by Arg-Ag@Cu attributed to the functionalization by Cu (II) was evaluated. The produced ROS partly derived from the CDT of Cu (II) through Fenton-like reaction, which could catalyze H2O2 to form highly toxic ·OH for inducing tumor cells apoptosis and abundant O2 for alleviating hypoxia in TME [36,74]. In addition, the functionalization of Cu (II) also modulated the band structure of CQDs, resulting in an emerging absorbance peak around 650 nm, guaranteed the 660 nm-induced photodynamic therapy (PDT), thus further facilitate ROS accumulation. Specifically, the production of ROS by CDT and PDT effect was reciprocally complete and promote each other, thus representing synergistic effects in following therapeutic procedure.

Fig. 3 A illustrated the process by which Arg-Ag@Cu achieved CDT and PDT effects in response to internal stimuli of TME. Although widely used in tumor therapy, PDT is heavily dependent on O2 in essence, while the TME is hypoxia, thus significantly inhibiting PDT efficiency [75]. Fenton-like reaction could act as the catalyst to produce O2 in excess of H2O2, offering Fenton agents great potentials for modulating TME to improve the therapeutic effects of PDT [76]. Thus, we first determined whether Arg-Ag@Cu could act as an efficient Fenton-like agent to catalyze H2O2 and consequently produce abundant O2 in a short time, which would be a promising strategy for improving the antitumor effect of PDT effect. As shown in Fig. 3B, Arg-Ag@Cu could generate O2 after mixing with H2O2 through Fenton-like reaction and is five-fold more abundant than H2O2 alone, which confirmed Cu (II) ions exhibited considerable catalase-like activity in modulating the hypoxia in TME.

The feasibility of Arg-Ag@Cu as photosensitizer for PDT was assessed by 1O2 sensor agent 1, 3-diphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF, Fig. 3C). In the absence of Arg-Ag@Cu, the degradation of DPBF incubated with H2O2 was almost negligible, which means H2O2 alone almost cannot produce considerable 1O2 in a short time. The significant degradation of DPBF occurred after the addition of Arg-Ag@Cu into H2O2 solution, with the absorbance of DPBF at 420 nm promptly decreased, owing to the more O2 generation by Fenton-like reation, achiveing improved PDT. The degradation of DPBF was further aggravated when laser added, suggesting the effective PDT effect achieved by Arg-Ag@Cu. Mechanistically, laser irradiation could enhance the electronic transition of Cu (II) ions thus promote the generation of ROS. In Fenton-like reaction, intracellular GSH can reduce Cu (II) to more active Cu (I) while being consumed during this process, thus achieving more effective ROS production while making cells more susceptible to other therapeutic agents [77]. Therefore, when mixing Arg-Ag@Cu with GSH in advance (completely converted Cu (II) to Cu (I)), and the DPBF degradation of Arg-Ag@Cu–H2O2 mixture under laser irradiation was found to be much significant than that of other groups, verifying the efficient H2O2-responsive generation of 1O2 by Arg-Ag@Cu. In addition, from the required morphology observation of TEM (Fig. S11), after mixing with GSH, the structure of Arg-Ag@Cu was disrupted and lost the nanoparticle form, which not only provided sufficient evidence for the reaction between Cu (II) and GSH for Fenton-like effect, but also was the basis of drug release in TME in the subsequent experimental procedures.

Subsequently, the role of Arg-Ag@Cu in Fenton-like reaction was further tested by MB, which is an indicator to detect the formation of ·OH, the typical product of Fenton-like reaction. The results were generally consistent with 1O2 generation. As shown in Fig. 3D, when mixing Arg-Ag@Cu with GSH to completely converted Cu (II) to Cu (I), the MB degradation of Arg-Ag@Cu–H2O2 mixture under laser irradiation was found to be much significant than that of other groups, verifying the efficient H2O2-responsive generation of ·OH and consequent CDT capacity of Arg-Ag@Cu. Intracellular ROS production of Arg-Ag@Cu was further evaluated by DCFH-DA using CLSM, which can be oxidized by ROS to yield green fluorescent DCF (λex = 525 nm) for ROS monitoring (Fig. 3E). As expected, the H2O2 or Arg-Ag@Cu alone group has a negligible green ﬂuorescence which was hardly observed by CLSM. The fluorescence of Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + GSH group was 1.2-fold stronger than Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 group, due to the stronger catalyze ability of Cu (I) ions which were reduced by GSH from Cu (II) ions. In contrast, the cells treated with Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + GSH + laser showed the strongest green fluorescence (∼7.1-fold stronger than control group), which suggested more ROS was produced by laser-assisted Fenton-like reaction through PDT effect (Fig. 3F). Such results manifested that the functionalization of Cu (II) enables efficient ROS replenishment via CDT and PDT, respectively, which paved a way for subsequent therapeutic procedures.

CQDs have gained interests among researchers due to their excellent drug delivery potential. Loading clinical standard chemotherapeutic drug DOX into Arg-Ag@Cu will not only resolve the poor water solubility and biosafety of DOX, but also relieve the drug resistance due to the Fenton-like reaction of Cu (II) through consuming high GSH in TME (Fig. 4 A). In addition, unlike traditional CQDs-based drug delivery systems, the particle size of Arg-Ag@Cu was evidently enlarged by Cu (II) functionalization and subsequent self-assembly, which was expected to address the limitations of CQDs attributed to the uncontrollable ultrasmall particle size, thus meeting satisfactory therapeutic effects. The characteristic peak of DOX (a and b) which could be observe from the absorption spectra of Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX (a’ and b’), as well as the following visible color changes manifesting that DOX was successfully uploaded (Fig. 4B). Interestingly, after loading DOX with Arg-Ag@Cu, both the characteristic absorption peaks of DOX (a and b) and Arg-Ag@Cu (c) were shifted to some extent. In general, hydrophobic forces may cause the shift or superposition of spectra [78], and this phenomenon was also observed in our previous study [11,79], which is an indication of hydrophobic encapsulation of DOX by Arg-Ag@Cu nanocomposites. The binding of DOX to Arg-Ag@Cu was further verified by fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4C). At 480 nm excitation, the fluorescence peak of DOX and Arg-Ag@Cu appeared at 598 nm and 516 nm (the fluorescence spectra of Arg-Ag@Cu with the excitation light ranged from 420 to 600 nm were shown in Fig. S12), and they both appeared in the fluorescence spectra of Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX. The loading of DOX in Arg-Ag@Cu mainly attributed to hydrophobic interactions, and the π-π stacking of the DOX in the hydrophobic cavity could form H-aggregates, which would remarkably reduce fluorescence [80,81]. This may also explain the weaker PL emission of DOX in Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX compared with free DOX (Fig. 4C). In addition, the weak PL emission of Arg-Ag@Cu (PL peak = 516 nm) might cause by the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between CQDs and DOX (namely, the “emitted” fluorescence of CQDs is transferred to excite DOX, resulting in the fluorescence emission of DOX) [79,82]. These observations further demonstrated the successfully uploaded of DOX. The drug loading was calculated by DOX standard curve (Fig. S13). By calculation, the drug-loading content was ∼0.4236 mg/mL.

Although the EPR effect plays an indispensable role in the field of nanomedicine-based targeted therapies, in order to obtain better therapeutic effects as well as reduced systemic toxicity, we next impelled biomimetic construction by coating Arg-Ag@Cu/Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX with M1 macrophages-derived microvesicles (MMVs) to improve active target efficiency, simultaneously. Most studies have emphasized that the microvesicles have the same membrane proteins as donor cells, which can activate the same signal transduction [83]. These microvesicles therefore have the ability of homotypic targeting to specific cells or tissues [84] and likely serve as an ideal natural drug carrier with low immunogenicity [85]. In addition, researchers have already applied macrophage membranes to construct biomimetic drug delivery systems for tumor targeted therapy [86,87]. Macrophages have been classified into two different phenotypes: classically activated macrophages, namely, M1 macrophages and alternatively activated macrophages, also called M2 macrophages [88]. Specifically, M1 macrophages are highly differentiated and have the tropism of recognizing then capturing tumor cells [89]. However, using M1 macrophages as tumor-targeted drug delivery vehicles may cause cell proliferation and metastasis in vivo. Therefore, using microvesicles derived from M1 macrophages (MMVs) as the vehicle for drug delivering appeared to be feasible in not only inheriting the tumor-targeting ability of M1 macrophages, but also avoiding the risk of introducing live cells.

To discriminate the phenotype of macrophages we used in the following experiments, we first conducted cell staining and FCM analysis. Total macrophages were identified according to the dual presence of CD45 (leukocytes common antigen) [90] and F4/80 (major macrophage marker) [91], followed by staining of CD80 (M1 marker) [92]. As shown in Fig. S14 and 99.25% total macrophages account for M1 macrophages, laying a solid foundation for later experimental procedures. Then, cytochalasin B (CB) was used to prepare MMVs, as it is a cell-permeable mycotoxin which can block the formation of contractile microfilaments thus promoting the secretion of microvesicles (Fig. 4D) [47]. As shown in Fig. 4E, CB induced MMVs were about 40 nm in diameter due to the dehydration effect occurred during the sample preparation. MMVs were mainly composed of membrane structures, and the successful construction of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu was preliminarily defined by the presence of a transparent membranous structure around it (Fig. 4F). After centrifugation to completely remove free MMVs, we conducted SDS-PAGE electrophoresis assay and western blot (WB) analysis to further demonstrate the successful construction of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu. Based on previous researches, membrane proteins play a dominant role in the biofunctions of various functional cells [93]. Thus, the transfer of membrane proteins from M1 macrophages to MMVs and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu is essential to their tumor-targeted ability, which was worth exploring. First, SDS-PAGE electrophoresis assay showed that purified MMVs, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu and M1 macrophages had the same total protein expression spectrum (Fig. 4G). We further conducted WB analysis to estimate the expression of α4 integrins and CD44 in MMVs, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu and M1 macrophages (Fig. 4H). The β-action signal was used as a control in these samples. The signals of α4 integrin and CD44 appeared in MMVs, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu and M1 macrophages, while no protein signal was presented in the Arg-Ag@Cu group. The above results showed that α4 integrin and CD44 were resisting on the MMVs, and these proteins can transfer to MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu through biomimetic construction. The α4 integrins expressed on the surfaces of macrophages are closely related to tumor metastasis [94]. Meanwhile, it was previously reported that the tumor-targeted mechanism of MMVs was attributed to CD44 expressed on their surfaces, which is similar to M1 macrophages [95]. Therefore, these two proteins were expressed on MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu was associated with the success of biomimetic construction and the efficient tumor-targeted ability, subsequently. The zeta potential of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu was found to be −10.18 mV, while Arg-Ag@Cu was −0.86, due to the negative charge on MMVs transferred to MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu, associating with the success of biomimetic construction (Fig. 4I). Meanwhile, the MMVs-coating did not affect the UV–vis characteristic absorption peak and photoluminescence properties of Arg-Ag@Cu (Fig. S15). In addition, to confirm that the biomimetic construction would not affect Arg-Ag@Cu to exert the CDT/PDT effect, DPBF, MB, and DCFH-DA were used to confirm the 1O2, ·OH and ROS producing capacity of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu. These results showed that Arg-Ag@Cu could exerted CDT/PDT effects after biomimetic construction under in vitro simulated TME condition (Fig. S16).

MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX was synthesized and purified by the same procedure as described above. From CLSM observations, the green fluorescence (DiO, representing MMVs) was co-localized with the red fluorescence (DOX), revealed the successful construction of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX (Fig. S17). In addition, to investigate the drug release behaviors of Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX by the sequential TME stimuli, each sample was analyzed by a dialysis method at different buffered media containing GSH and H2O2 to simulate the reduction and oxidation conditions of TME. In the simulated TME condition (with the addition of GSH and H2O2, pH = 5), the release of DOX was ∼94%, while only ∼ 4% in the neutral condition (pH = 7). Compared with Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX took longer to reach ∼94% release (12 h–24 h) under the same condition, which can ensure the continuous treatment in the TME (Fig. S18). Theoretically, the Fenton-like reaction is only efficient in acidic conditions, while relatively inefficient under physiological neutral conditions [96]. In addition, the protonation effect of DOX under acidic conditions also enhanced the drug release. Meanwhile, the lifetime of H2O2 and GSH was also highly affected by the pH, they have poor stability in alkaline and neutral conditions and the acidic condition was preferred [97]. In summary, only in acid condition, Cu (II) could react with H2O2 and GSH to fully achieve the Fenton-like reaction and dissociated from the Arg-Ag@Cu nanocomplexes, resulting in the disassembly of the nanocomplexes, followed by the subsequent drug release. These results suggested that Arg-Ag@Cu and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu could be effective vehicles for loading and releasing DOX.

Encouraged by the present studies which demonstrated MMVs as a promising vehicle with which to mimic macrophages, we next constructed a breast cancer lung metastasis model in ICR mice to determine whether MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu had a stronger tumor-targeted ability compared with Arg-Ag@Cu. In order to accurately observe the systemic distribution of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu in vivo, it was loaded with ICG while Arg-Ag@Cu was undergoing the same process (Fig. S19). ICR mice with breast cancer lung metastasis were randomly divided into two groups, then Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG were injected into the tail-vein of the mice, separately (n = 3/group). The near-infrared (NIR) small animal imaging system was used to observe the fluorescence signal of ICG in each group, thereby monitoring the biodistribution of Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG in mice. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, both Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG began to accumulate in tumor tissues at 30 min after intravenous injection. The fluorescence signal in tumor tissues peaked at 4 h after Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG injection. In contrast, the signal peaked at 6 h after MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG injection. Interestingly, we observed that MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG treatment rendered a significantly stronger signal in the tumor region (ten-fold or so at 6 h after injection) (Fig. 5B) and sustained a longer time (over 36 h) compared with Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG treatment (24 h). Indicating MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG had a more accurate accumulation in tumor region and the longer circulation time in vivo. In order to further examined the distribution of Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG in vivo, the mice were sacrificed at 6 h, respectively. The fluorescence signals of the main organs were recorded (Fig. 5C). The fluorescence of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG was mainly distributed in the livers and lungs, while the fluorescence signal of Arg-Ag@Cu-ICG basically appeared in the livers. Due to the as-prepared Arg-Ag@Cu had the similar photoluminesecne properties which were inherited from Arg-Ag CQDs, we determined whether the fluorescence signal of Arg-Ag@Cu itself could be observed in tumor issues using GFP channel (λex = 390–490 nm, λem = 500–550 nm). As shown in Fig. S20, the tumor-bearing ICR mice were sacrificed at 24 h after Arg-Ag@Cu intravenous injection and the significant fluorescence signal of Arg-Ag@Cu appears in the lungs, indicating the tumor-target ability of Arg-Ag@Cu due to the EPR effect. Taken together, these results suggested that although Arg-Ag@Cu can enter tumor tissues through EPR effect, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu can achieve more accurate target location and effective lung drug accumulation through active targeting combined with NO-enhanced EPR effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The therapeutic effect of Arg-Ag@Cu and the possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: It has been confirmed that Arg-Ag@Cu has fully exerted the intrinsic therapeutic effects of CQDs, namely RNS and ROS generation, and both of which may contribute to tumor cell apoptosis or necroptosis. To identify the specific anticancer mechanisms of Arg-Ag@Cu, we first adopted Annexin V/PI staining to distinguish the different apoptosis stages attributed to Arg-Ag@Cu. 4T1 cells were incubated with Arg-Ag@Cu for 12 h and evaluated by FCM. As shown in Fig. 6 A and B, after treated with Arg-Ag@Cu, the apoptotic percentage of 4T1 cells (including early apoptosis and late apoptosis) increased from 32.17% (control, 16.09% for early apoptosis and 16.08% for late apoptosis) to 42.93% (Arg-Ag@Cu, 11.05% for early apoptosis and 31.88% for late apoptosis), 48.58% (Arg-Ag@Cu + Laser, 10.25% for early apoptosis and 38.33% for late apoptosis, 57.81% (Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2, 31.00% for early apoptosis and 26.81% for late apoptosis), and 87.77% (Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + Laser, 57.95% for early apoptosis and 29.82% for late apoptosis). These results demonstrated that Arg-Ag@Cu can induce cell apoptosis rather than necrosis, which may result in inflammation thus leading to a variety of consequences.

To further identify the anticancer mechanisms of Arg-Ag@Cu, we quantified apoptosis-associated proteins including caspase 3, Bcl-2 and Bax using WB assay (Fig. 6C and D) [98]. In comparison with the control group, the expression of caspase 3 and Bax showed a gradually increase in Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2, Arg-Ag@Cu + Laser and Arg-Ag@Cu + H2O2 + Laser treatment groups, while the expression of Bcl-2 showed a completely opposite trend. Pro-apoptotic BAX is the major executioner member of the BCL-2 family that, through conformational activation and oligomerization at the outer mitochondrial membrane (MOM), causes permeabilization of the MOM and release of mitochondrial factors, which activate the caspase cascade of apoptosis [99]. Caspase 3 serves as another marker for mitochondria-mediated apoptosis, and Bcl-2 is the classic mediator of anti-apoptosis [100]. Their expression changes collectively illustrated that the apoptosis pathway was activated. To sum up, Annexin V/PI staining experiments and WB assay illustrated that Arg-Ag@Cu could induce cell death via apoptosis, indicating that the generated ROS derived from CDT effect together with RNS can assist the antitumor effect of laser-induced PDT, which synergistically contributed to multi-functional tumor theranostics.

The effective apoptosis-inducing ability as well as the DOX-loading efficiency of Arg-Ag@Cu together with the homotypic-targeting ability of MMVs guaranteed the further treatment of 4T1 tumor-bearing mice. To corroborate the therapeutic effect of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, ICR mice with breast cancer lung metastasis were randomly divided into five groups, and DOX was chosen as clinical standard treatment. First, the hemolysis assay of Arg-Ag@Cu, Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX were conducted to demonstrate their potential as highly biocompatible antitumor nano-devices (Fig. S21). PBS, DOX, Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX (200 μL) were injected into the tail-vein of the mice, separately. In addition, the last group was undergone a 10 min laser radiation to complete the whole treatment (Fig. 6E). As shown in Fig. 6F, after two weeks of treatment, a large area of tumor necrosis tissue was found in the lungs of mice treated with PBS and slight inhibition of lung metastasis was observed in mice treated with DOX (∼6% decrease compared with PBS group). Fig. 6G showed the detailed scheme of the therapeutic procedure as well as related mechanisms. From the quantitative analysis of tumor/tissue in Fig. 6H, MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX group showed a more obvious therapeutic effect (∼20% decrease compared with PBS group) than the DOX and Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX groups (∼9% decrease compared with PBS group). It has demonstrated that the biomimetic procedure endowed MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX the ability to target lung metastasis sites, thereby achieving TEM-responsive RNS and ROS accumulation as well as efficiently DOX release. In particular, the area of tumor necrosis in the lungs of mice in MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX + Laser group was negligible, which was ∼23% decrease compared with PBS group. Additionally, the weight of mice in each group did not significantly loss throughout the experimental period (Fig. 6I). These results illustrated the synergistic performance of the CQDs-based drug delivery platform including CDT, PDT, NO-based RNS and chemotherapy can achieve the most optimal therapeutic effect with minimal side effects. In order to investigate the biosafety of MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, H&E analysis was performed on normal organs (Fig. S22). The results showed that MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX had no obvious toxicity to the heart, liver, spleens, lung and kidney throughout our studies. From the results of H&E staining in lungs, obvious tumor lesions can be seen in PBS, DOX, Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX, and MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX groups. In contrast, the tumor lesions were significantly reduced after MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX + Laser treatment, further indicating that the biomimetic construction enabled MMV-Arg-Ag@Cu-DOX to actively target to lung metastasis site, thereby increasing the accumulation of drugs in the tumor area (Fig. 6J).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of Arg-Ag@Cu on 4T1 cells migration and the possible mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Copper-based nanocatalysts have been suffering from severe queries as inducing tumor metastasis [42]. In the meantime, NO has been widely reported to down-regulated matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2 and MMP-9) [29,30], which is related to tumor invasion and metastasis. Consequently, the comprehensive effects of Arg-Ag@Cu on 4T1 cells migration necessitated further investigation. First, a wound healing assay was adopted, which is commonly used to evaluate cell migration ability in tumor research. The cell migration ability was evaluated by observing the wound healing percentage between the scratch of the control group and the experimental group, and the most common index was the area of scratched wound. The results showed that in the comparison with PBS group, the cells treated with Arg-Ag@Cu migrated more slowly to close the scratched wound (Fig. 7 A). From the quantitative results in Fig. 7B, the area of scratched wound in Arg-Ag@Cu group was one-third larger than PBS group after 24 h incubation. Furthermore, a transwell assay was performed. Comparing with the PBS group, the number of migrated 4T1 cells incubated with Arg-Ag@Cu was significantly declined (Fig. 7C) and the results of OD value measurement of crystal violet eluate showed that PBS group was about 2.5 times that of Arg-Ag@Cu treatment group (Fig. 7D). In summary, these results indicated that the incubation of Arg-Ag@Cu could inhibit the migration of 4T1 cells in vitro. Since the expression of MMPs is essential for cell migration, the effect of Arg-Ag@Cu on MMPs was investigated by WB (Fig. 7E and F). Arg-Ag@Cu treatment resulted in a decrease of MMP-9/MMP-2 expression accompanied by an increase of TIMP-1/TIMP-2 expression, the negative regulators of MMPs [101], demonstrated that Arg-Ag@Cu can affect the expression of proteolytic activation-related proteins thus inhibit the migration of 4T1 cells. In summary, these results attributed to the property of Arg-Ag@Cu-produced NO to down-regulate the expression of MMP-2 and MMP-9.

The inhibition of Arg-Ag@Cu on breast cancer lung metastasis model was further investigated. The injection of 4T1 cells into tail-vein leads to the metastasis of tumor in the lungs of mice. 5 days later, the mice were treated with Arg-Ag@Cu for 9 days through intravenous injection (Fig. 7G). It can be seen in Fig. 7H, Arg-Ag@Cu treatment significantly reduced the lung tumor necrosis area (∼7%) compared with PBS group (∼55%) (Fig. 7I). In addition, from the results of tissue H&E staining, the tumor lesions were significantly lessened after Arg-Ag@Cu treatment, indicating its excellent therapeutic effect (Fig. 7J). The body weight of each mouse was not affected by 4T1 cells injection or Arg-Ag@Cu administration (Fig. 7K). Meanwhile, acute toxicity on non-target organs was determined using liver injury indicators AST and ALT (Fig. S23). Collectively, all these results expounded Arg-Ag@Cu as a highly safe and effective platform for tumor therapy.

In conclusion, we have developed duplex metal co-doped CQDs-based drug delivery system (Arg-Ag@Cu), which fully exerted the therapeutic performances of CQDs and their applications in targeted drug delivery systems through doping metallic dopant Ag, retaining residues of precursor l-arginine, and further functionalizing by Cu (II). Specifically, Ag-doping dramatically enhanced the fluorescence intensity of l-arginine-based CQDs, the guanidinium residues guaranteed the RNS (NO and ONOO−) supplement and the amino residues ensured the binding of Cu (II). Above all, Cu (II) played decisive roles: a catalytic role for releasing ROS through CDT effect while consuming GSH to guarantee chemotherapy, a mediated role for modulating the band structure of as-prepared CQDs and subsequent laser-induced photodynamic therapy (PDT), and a crucial role for intelligent regulating the particle size of CQDs. On this basis, after loading DOX and coating MMVs, the as-prepared CQDs were highly targeted to the tumor site, and along with the depletion of Cu (II), in situ DOX release was successfully achieved. Finally, the as-prepared CQDs-based drug delivery system significantly suppressed tumor progression and metastasis with minimal side effects. To the best of our knowledge, this study is a promising attempt that fully exerts the bio-function of CQDs themselves and further acts as a drug delivery vehicle, and paves a new way for carbonaceous materials-based antitumor theranostics.
